title: Gazebo & Outdoor Kitchen Installation on Concrete Footings in Lattingtown, Long Island
seoTitle: Gazebo & Outdoor Kitchen Installation on Concrete Footings — Lattingtown, Long Island, NY | Repair Asap LLC
description: Repair Asap built a backyard gazebo and outdoor kitchen on poured concrete footings in the Locust Valley / Lattingtown area of Nassau County, Long Island — lifting pavers, drilling and pouring footings under each post, then assembling the frame, roof, drainage, and kitchen over three working days.
excerpt: An existing paver patio became a permanent outdoor living space. Repair Asap lifted pavers, drilled and poured concrete footings under each post, then a two-person crew assembled the gazebo frame, roof, drainage, and an outdoor kitchen.
location: Locust Valley / Lattingtown, Nassau County, NY
scope: Lifting existing pavers at each gazebo post location
Drilling and excavating footing holes in the ground below
Setting a gravel base and formwork for each footing
Pouring concrete footing pads under every gazebo leg
Curing time before any structure was assembled
Two-person gazebo frame assembly by the manufacturer's instructions
Roof panel installation and roof-edge detailing
Fitting the gazebo's integrated drainage / downspout components
Outdoor kitchen unit placement and assembly
Resetting pavers and final cleanup around the structure
challenge: The homeowner wanted a permanent, freestanding gazebo and outdoor kitchen on an existing paver patio. A kit gazebo of this size can't simply sit on pavers — each post needed a solid concrete footing below grade so the structure would stay level and stable over time, which meant working carefully around the finished patio.
solution: Repair Asap lifted the pavers at each post location, drilled and excavated the footing holes, set gravel and formwork, and poured a concrete footing pad under every leg. After the concrete cured, a two-person crew assembled the gazebo frame and roof by the manufacturer's instructions, fitted the roof drainage and downspout components, installed the outdoor kitchen, and reset and cleaned the patio.
result: Over three working days, a flat paver patio became a finished outdoor living space: a level, footing-anchored gazebo with a hardtop roof, integrated drainage, and an outdoor kitchen. The foundation plus the complete installation were delivered for a project total of $4,500. Final pricing depends on size, site conditions, foundation requirements, access, materials, utilities, and scope.

content.startingTitle: An existing paver patio ready for a permanent gazebo
startingParagraphs: The project started on a finished paver patio in the Locust Valley / Lattingtown area of Nassau County, Long Island. The homeowner wanted a large freestanding gazebo with an outdoor kitchen as a permanent backyard feature.

A structure this size needs real support. Rather than setting the posts on top of the pavers, each leg would sit on its own poured concrete footing below grade, so the gazebo stays level and stable through the seasons.
content.beforeTitle: Before: existing paver patio and post-location prep
content.beforeSubtitle: The work began on a finished paver patio. Pavers were lifted at each post location to open up the ground for footings.
content.processTitle: Concrete footings first, then a two-person gazebo build
processParagraphs: Day one was foundation work. The crew lifted the pavers at each post location, drilled and excavated the footing holes, and set a gravel base with formwork for each footing.
Concrete was poured for a footing pad under every gazebo leg, with anchor hardware set into the concrete. The site was then left so the concrete could cure before any weight went on it.

Once the footings had cured, a two-person crew returned and assembled the gazebo frame and roof following the manufacturer's instructions, then completed the roof detailing and fitted the integrated drainage and downspout components.

The final day covered the outdoor kitchen installation and a full cleanup, including resetting the pavers around the new posts. The foundation plus the complete build was delivered for a project total of $4,500 — final pricing depends on size, site conditions, foundation requirements, access, materials, utilities, and scope.
content.processGalleryTitle: Process: concrete footings, framing, and roof
content.processGallerySubtitle: Selected progress photos from footing holes and poured concrete pads through frame and roof assembly.
content.detailSubtitle: Each gazebo post lands on its own footing and base plate, set flush with the surrounding pavers.
content.afterTitle: After: finished gazebo and outdoor kitchen
content.afterSubtitle: The completed structure — a hardtop gazebo on concrete footings with an outdoor kitchen — ready for use on the existing patio.
content.servicesTitle: Planning a gazebo or outdoor project?
content.servicesSubtitle: This outdoor build connects to several Repair ASAP service areas across Long Island and NYC.
content.ctaTitle: Planning a gazebo, pergola, or outdoor kitchen?
content.ctaText: Text photos of your patio or yard for a fast estimate, and Repair ASAP will help plan the foundation and the installation.

faqs.q: How long does gazebo installation take?
faqs.a: It depends on the size and the foundation. This project ran about three working days: one day for concrete footings, curing time in between, then frame, roof, drainage, and outdoor kitchen assembly with a two-person crew.

Larger freestanding and hardtop gazebos are most stable on concrete footings poured below grade under each post. Smaller units can sometimes be anchored differently, but footings help keep a big structure level and secure over time.

faqs.q: Can you install a gazebo on or around an existing paver patio?
faqs.a: Yes. On this project the pavers were lifted at each post location so footings could be poured, then reset around the finished posts, so the patio stays usable and the gazebo still gets a solid foundation.

faqs.q: Can you install outdoor kitchen kits?
faqs.a: Yes. We assemble and place outdoor kitchen islands and grill units as part of an outdoor build. Any gas, water, or electrical hookups are handled separately based on the specific unit and site.

faqs.q: How do I get a quote for an outdoor project?
faqs.a: Text or send photos of your patio or yard and describe what you want to add. We review access, site conditions, and foundation needs, then give you an estimate. Final pricing depends on size, site conditions, foundation requirements, access, materials, utilities, and scope.

title: Bathroom Build-Out From Rough Plumbing to a Tiled Marble Shower in Queens
seoTitle: Bathroom Build-Out From Rough Plumbing to Tiled Shower in Queens, NY | Repair Asap LLC
description: Repair Asap turned a bathroom that was only rough-plumbed — water supply, drain, and ventilation with no finished walls or ceiling — into a fully tiled marble-look shower and bathroom in St. Albans, Queens, with cement backer board, waterproofing, a sloped shower pan, and wall and floor tile.
excerpt: This bathroom started as bare framing with only rough plumbing and utilities. Repair Asap built the walls and ceiling, waterproofed the wet areas, formed a sloped shower pan, and tiled the shower and floor in marble-look porcelain and hexagon mosaic.
scope: Framing and wall build-out over rough plumbing and utilities
Cement backer board for the wet-area walls
Waterproofing of the shower walls and floor
Sloped shower pan / base built down to the drain
Large-format marble-look porcelain wall tile
Hexagon mosaic shower and floor tile
Built-in shower bench and recessed niche
Tile cutting and fitting around valves, corners, and drains
challenge: The bathroom existed only as rough-in: water supply, a drain/sewer line, and ventilation were in place, but there were no finished walls, no ceiling, and no shower. The space was bare framing, exposed brick, and open joists.
solution: Repair Asap framed and boarded the walls and ceiling, installed cement backer board and waterproofing in the wet areas, floated a shower pan that slopes evenly to the drain, then set large-format marble-look porcelain on the walls and a white hexagon mosaic on the shower floor, with a built-in bench and recessed niche.
result: A room that was only rough plumbing and bare studs became a fully tiled, marble-look bathroom with a waterproofed, properly sloped shower — turning an unfinished utility space into a finished wet room.

content.startingTitle: Only rough plumbing and utilities — no walls, no ceiling, no shower
startingParagraphs: When the project started, the bathroom was just rough-in: water supply lines, a drain/sewer connection, and ventilation against bare framing, exposed brick, and open ceiling joists.
There were no finished walls, no ceiling, and no shower. Everything that makes a bathroom usable still had to be built, waterproofed, and tiled from scratch.
content.beforeTitle: Before: bare framing over rough plumbing
content.beforeSubtitle: The room began as rough utilities only — supply lines, drain, and ventilation with no finished surfaces.
content.processTitle: Backer board, waterproofing, a sloped shower pan, and tile
processParagraphs: Repair Asap closed in the walls and ceiling, then installed cement backer board across the wet-area walls and added waterproofing where the shower would go.
A shower pan was floated and checked with a level so it slopes evenly to the drain — the part of the job that keeps a shower watertight for the long run.
With the substrate ready, large-format marble-look porcelain was set on the walls and a white hexagon mosaic on the shower floor, with tiles cut and fit around the valve, corners, bench, and niche.
content.processGalleryTitle: Process: from backer board and waterproofing to tile
content.processGallerySubtitle: Selected progress photos show the cement board, waterproofing, sloped shower pan, and marble-look tile going in.
content.afterTitle: After: a tiled marble-look shower and bathroom
content.afterSubtitle: The finished wet area reads clean and premium — marble-look walls, a hexagon mosaic floor, a built-in bench, and a recessed niche.
content.servicesTitle: Planning a bathroom build-out or shower tile job?
content.servicesSubtitle: This project connects to tile and flooring, drywall, plumbing, bathroom fixtures, and waterproofing/caulking work.
content.ctaTitle: Have a rough-plumbed or unfinished bathroom in NYC?
content.ctaText: Send photos of the space and Repair ASAP can help build it out — walls, waterproofing, shower pan, and tile.

title: Crown Royal Barbershop Renovation in Manhattan
seoTitle: Commercial Barbershop Renovation in Manhattan, NY | Repair Asap LLC
description: Repair Asap completed a commercial barbershop build-out for Crown Royal Barbershop on 3rd Ave in Manhattan, including drywall repair, restroom build-out, ceiling leak repair, porcelain floor tile, subway tile, painting, lighting, and a custom wood slat partition.
excerpt: A damaged Manhattan commercial space was turned into a finished barbershop with new drywall, restroom tile, porcelain flooring, subway tile, lighting, painting, and a custom wood slat feature wall.
scope: Restroom drywall and ceiling build-out
Commercial drywall repair and wall leveling
Ceiling repair after upstairs water leak
Porcelain floor tile installation
Bathroom tile installation
Subway tile installation in the main room
Interior painting and finish prep
Light fixture installation
Drywall partition with vertical wood slat panels and integrated accent lighting
Final shelving, artwork, and touch-ups
challenge: The barbershop space had old damaged drywall from previous tenants, an unfinished restroom with only rough plumbing, missing ceiling sections, leak-damaged ceiling areas, and uneven walls throughout the customer-facing room.
solution: Repair Asap rebuilt the restroom walls and ceiling, repaired and leveled drywall across the main space, installed durable porcelain floor tile and subway wall tile, restored leak-damaged ceiling sections with an access panel, painted the interior, installed lighting, and built a custom wood slat partition with integrated accent lighting.
result: The project delivered a clean, durable, customer-ready barbershop interior with a finished restroom, tile floors and walls, repaired ceilings, professional lighting, and a warm custom feature wall.

title: Drywall Repair After Mini-Split Installation in Brooklyn
seoTitle: Drywall Repair After Mini-Split AC Installation in Brooklyn, NY | Repair Asap LLC
description: Repair Asap restored drywall around an indoor mini-split AC unit in East Williamsburg, Brooklyn after the wall was cut during installation, then patched, compounded, dried, and painted the area for a clean finish.
excerpt: After a mini-split indoor AC unit was installed, the wall below it had open drywall cuts and exposed rough edges. Repair Asap patched, compounded, dried, and painted the wall for a clean finished look.
scope: Drywall opening assessment after mini-split installation
Patch sizing and fitting around the AC lines and wall opening
Drywall patch fastening and surface preparation
Joint compound and skim-coat work
Drying time between repair stages
Interior wall painting and final cleanup
challenge: The indoor mini-split AC unit was already installed, but the wall below and around it had open drywall cuts, exposed edges, and an unfinished repair area that needed to be closed cleanly without disturbing the unit.
solution: Repair Asap measured and fit drywall patch pieces, fastened the repair around the AC lines, applied joint compound, allowed the surface to dry, sanded/prepped the area, and painted the wall so the installation looked finished.
result: The wall around the mini-split unit was restored from an open cutout to a clean painted surface, improving the room's appearance while keeping access and lines properly accommodated.

content.startingTitle: Open drywall cuts after mini-split AC installation
startingParagraphs: The indoor split AC unit was in place, but the surrounding drywall was left cut open with exposed edges and rough access areas below the unit.
This kind of repair needs careful patching because the wall has to be closed neatly around existing AC lines without creating a bulky or uneven finish.
content.beforeTitle: Before: open drywall around the AC unit
content.beforeSubtitle: The repair started with visible cutouts, rough drywall edges, and an unfinished wall below the indoor mini-split unit.
content.processTitle: Patch, compound, dry, prep, and paint
processParagraphs: Repair Asap measured the damaged area, cut and fit drywall patch pieces, and fastened the wall repair around the existing mini-split lines.
The patch was covered with joint compound and allowed to dry between stages before the surface was prepped for paint.
After painting, the wall read as a finished installation instead of an open construction cutout.
content.processGalleryTitle: Process: fitting and finishing the drywall patch
content.processGallerySubtitle: Selected progress photos show measuring, patch installation, compound work, and paint prep around the mini-split unit.
content.afterTitle: After: clean painted wall around the mini-split
content.afterSubtitle: The finished wall surface looks clean and intentional around the indoor AC unit.
content.servicesTitle: Need drywall repair after AC installation?
content.servicesSubtitle: This project connects to drywall repair, interior painting, general handyman repairs, and AC-related wall finish work.
content.ctaTitle: Need a wall repaired after AC, plumbing, or electrical work?
content.ctaText: Send photos of the opening or damage and Repair ASAP can help patch, prep, and paint the wall.
